response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I never went to university or anything like that, but I see this issue clearly enough from where I stand. The gender gap in science is real and it bothers me because it means we're wasting half the potential talent out there. I work as a mechanic here in Rome, and honestly, in my shop it's almost all men too. But I've seen women who understand engines just as well as any guy, sometimes better. The same principle applies to science, right? When I help my kids with their homework, my daughters are just as curious about how things work as my sons. That curiosity shouldn't disappear just because society tells girls that science isn't for them. I think about my own life sometimes, how I never had the chance for formal education because of how things were in my family. Nobody pushed education on us. Now I'm raising five kids alone after losing my wife, and I worry about giving them opportunities I never had. If my daughters want to study physics or biology or whatever, I don't want them facing extra barriers just because they're girls. We absolutely should address this gap, though I'll admit I worry about whether anything really changes. Depression makes me pessimistic about most things these days, but this feels important enough to fight through that darkness. How do we fix it? Start young, I'd say. Make sure girls see women scientists as role models. In schools, encourage everyone equally. I've noticed when I'm baking with my kids or painting, they all enjoy it regardless of gender. We don't assign those activities to boys or girls, so why do we do it with science? Maybe it's also about showing that science isn't just for people with fancy degrees. I never finished school but I understand mechanical principles through practice. Science should be accessible to everyone who's curious. Companies and universities need to actively work against their own biases too. I know from scrolling social media occasionally that there are initiatives trying to support women in STEM fields. That seems like a good start, though implementation matters more than intentions. We need practical changes, not just talk.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 34-year-old widowed father of five working part-time as a mechanic in Rome with no formal education. His high openness suggests receptivity to progressive ideas about gender equality despite his traditional male-dominated occupation. His lack of formal education and parents' similar background means he approaches this from a practical, lived-experience perspective rather than academic. His depression and high neuroticism create a somewhat pessimistic but still engaged tone. His moderate agreeableness and extraversion suggest he's willing to express opinions but not aggressively. As a father of both sons and daughters, he has personal investment in fairness. His hobbies like baking and painting show he doesn't subscribe to rigid gender roles. His atheism removes religious objections to gender equality. The response reflects working-class perspective, personal family concerns, practical observations from his mechanic work, and cautious optimism tempered by depression.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective, somewhat melancholic but earnest, working-class practical perspective, personally invested due to fatherhood, cautiously supportive of change while battling pessimism from depression, conversational and unpretentious
